one_line_takeaway: A security-aware RAG pipeline that filters/aggregates SIEM logs via MITRE ATT&CK-mapped IOC queries before LLM retrieval is essential for incident analysis (no-RAG baselines miss all attack infrastructure), and once retrieval is properly configured (k=7), cross-chunk reasoning ability -- not deployment modality or security-specific pretraining -- is the dominant driver of LLM performance, letting DeepSeek V3 match Claude Sonnet 4's ~94% recall at 15x lower cost.
key_finding: On the 17-scenario malware benchmark, Claude Sonnet 4 achieves the highest average recall (94%), followed by DeepSeek V3 (89%), Llama 3.1:70b (81%), Cisco Foundation-Sec-8B (71%), and GPT-4/4o/5-mini/5.2 (64-69%). DeepSeek V3 is ~15x cheaper per analysis than Claude Sonnet 4 ($0.008 vs $0.12) while trailing by only 5 recall points; locally-deployed Llama 3.1:70b gets 81% recall at zero per-query cost. On the AD attack-reconstruction task, attack-step detection reaches 100% precision throughout and up to 96% recall (Claude Sonnet 4 and DeepSeek V3 tied) with an enumeration prompt; GPT variants ranged 46-69% recall on the same task. Cross-source integration analysis (Table 2) shows Claude reaches 89% recall on the 42 set-valued/multi-chunk questions and cites >=2 chunks in 95% of set-valued answers, versus 47-53% recall and 31-38% multi-chunk citation for GPT-family models -- the paper attributes model ranking primarily to cross-chunk synthesis ability rather than deployment modality or domain-specific pretraining (Cisco's security-specialized 8B model does not outperform general-purpose models). The context-size ablation (k in {1,3,5,7,14}) shows a crossover: at k=1 GPT models actually outperform Claude/DeepSeek (46% vs 39%/35%, because Claude answers 'Not found' rather than guessing from a single chunk), but by k=7 Claude leads by 27 points; k=14 shows no further gain and can slightly hurt (Cisco -4, Llama -3). RAG vs no-RAG on the fake_auth scenario: no-RAG models see only 0.6-4.3% of the 3,694 logs (chronologically first, benign-startup-biased) and miss all attack infrastructure, dropping Claude/DeepSeek recall from 100% (RAG) to 50%. Suricata-only recall is 40%, missing identity attribution (hostname, user account) entirely because IDS data lacks authentication records. Three disjoint failure-mode categories are identified with concrete fixes: query coverage (e.g., Elasticsearch top-N aggregation truncation silently dropped the 11th C2 IP on gozi), retrieval/size limits (raising k from 3->7 lifted Claude's dec21isc recall from 50%->100%; raising prompt char limit 20K->60K lifted jun21isc 27%->98%), and LLM reasoning errors (GPT-4 confusing a machine account desktop-e7fhjs4$ with the human user damon.bauer on qakbot; fixed by prompt wording).
core_contribution: The paper presents a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) system for security incident analysis that (1) runs a library of targeted IOC-extraction queries (mapped to MITRE ATT&CK techniques) against SIEM logs (Suricata alerts, Zeek connection records, Windows/Kerberos authentication events) to compress raw events into aggregated, semantically meaningful chunks, (2) embeds and indexes these chunks (all-mpnet-base-v2 + FAISS) for retrieval, and (3) prompts an LLM with the top-k retrieved chunks to answer forensic questions (identification, timeline, remediation) or reconstruct multi-step attack narratives. The system is evaluated end-to-end with eight LLM backends (Claude Sonnet 4, DeepSeek V3, GPT-4, GPT-4o, GPT-5-mini, GPT-5.2, locally-hosted Llama 3.1:70b, and security-specialized Cisco Foundation-Sec-8B) on 17 malware-traffic scenarios and one multi-stage Active Directory (AD) red-team attack, with ablations on retrieval depth k and comparisons against no-RAG and Suricata-only baselines.
threat_model: Not an adversarial/attack-defense threat model in the traditional sense; the paper's 'threat model' is operational: a Security Operations Center analyst must reconstruct what happened during a real intrusion (malware infection or a red-teamed AD compromise chain: Metasploit initial access, ESC1 certificate template abuse via SAN/Subject mismatch, Kerberos TGT/service-ticket forgery, PSExec lateral movement, malicious user creation, and SCADA service disruption) using SIEM logs that are too voluminous (a single scenario: 3,694 log events; overall corpus: 321 MB of PCAPs, 94,948 Security Onion log events) to fit in an LLM's context window. The system does not defend against attacks on the LLM/RAG pipeline itself (e.g., prompt injection via log content, retrieval poisoning) -- that surface is not discussed or evaluated.
limitations: The paper itself notes: (1) the IOC query library was developed iteratively and validated against the specific evaluated attack types, so extending to genuinely novel attack patterns requires manually adding new queries -- coverage is not automatic; (2) reference answer sets for the malware scenarios are drawn from published expert solutions but are explicitly 'not an exhaustive enumeration,' so recall (not precision) is the primary reported metric for that benchmark, and models may find legitimately suspicious indicators absent from the reference set (only Appendix H gives full precision numbers); (3) the AD evaluation is a single controlled red-team scenario, and the paper explicitly cautions that its 5-minute sliding-window choice was tuned to that attack's pace (core exploitation compressed into 60 seconds) and that window size must be retuned for slower (APT, multi-day lateral movement) or faster (ransomware) threats; (4) cost/pricing figures are noted as vendor pricing 'as of early 2026' and one model (GPT-4) uses substituted GPT-4.1 pricing because original pricing is no longer published; (5) no evaluation of adversarial robustness of the RAG/LLM pipeline itself (e.g., log-content prompt injection, retrieval poisoning, or IOC-query-evasion by an attacker aware of the query library) is performed.

datasets_used: 17 malware-traffic scenarios from Malware-Traffic-Analysis.net, Unit 42 Wireshark Quizzes, and SANS Internet Storm Center forensic contests (321 MB PCAPs, 94,948 Security Onion log events, 129 forensic questions, 218 reference indicators)
One multi-stage Active Directory red-team attack scenario (Security Onion/Kibana logs) with manually extracted reference timeline over 3 five-minute windows (R=11, 8, 7 reference steps respectively)
quantitative_results: Malware benchmark average recall: Claude Sonnet 4 94%, DeepSeek V3 89%, Llama 3.1:70b 81%, Cisco Foundation-Sec-8B 71%, GPT-4o 69%, GPT-5.2 68%, GPT-4 67%, GPT-5-mini 64%
AD attack-step detection: 100% precision for all models; recall up to 96% (Claude Sonnet 4 and DeepSeek V3 tied), down to 46% (GPT-5-mini)

Cross-source/set-valued question recall (Table 2): Claude 89% (95% multi-chunk citation rate), DeepSeek 78% (88%), Llama 56% (76%), Cisco 46% (36%), GPT-4 52% (38%), GPT-4o 53% (31%), GPT-5-mini 47% (33%), GPT-5.2 52% (33%)
Context-size ablation (k=1->3->5->7->14) recall for Claude: 39->72->86->94->94%; DeepSeek: 35->71->80->89->89%; GPTs gain only 13-18 points from k=1 to k=3 vs 33-36 points for Claude/DeepSeek
RAG vs no-RAG on fake_auth: Claude/DeepSeek 100% (RAG) vs 50% (no-RAG, seeing only 2.8-4.3% of 3,694 logs); GPT variants 56-69% (RAG) vs 38% (no-RAG, seeing 0.6% of logs)
Cost per analysis: Claude Sonnet 4 $0.12, DeepSeek V3 $0.008 (~15x cheaper), GPT-4 $0.11, GPT-4o $0.09, GPT-5.2 $0.09, GPT-5-mini $0.01, Llama/Cisco $0 (local, excludes GPU infra cost)
At 1,000 analyses/day operational scale: DeepSeek ~$240/month vs Claude ~$3,600/month
AD defense recommendation quality (Claude Sonnet 4): 89% average recall, 76% average precision across 3 windows
Average analysis time per scenario: Claude 100s, DeepSeek 110s, Llama 200s, Cisco 376s, GPT variants 17-19s
baselines_compared: No-RAG baseline (raw logs truncated to fit context window, no extraction/embedding)
Suricata-only baseline (IDS alerts alone, no cross-source log fusion)

8 LLM backends compared against each other as configurations of the same RAG pipeline: Claude Sonnet 4, DeepSeek V3, GPT-4, GPT-4o, GPT-5-mini, GPT-5.2, Llama 3.1:70b (local), Cisco Foundation-Sec-8B (local, security-specialized)
